to_premise_weak_quant: Socrates is a human being.
to_premise_weak_hedge: Socrates has various attributes typical of people.
notes: Removed the major premise (all humans are mortal); mortality no longer entailed, but nothing asserts Socrates is immortal, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: The number 14 results from doubling 7, and questions of parity concern divisibility by 2.
to_premise_weak_hedge: The number 14 has properties that are relevant to whether a number counts as even.
notes: Removed the explicit rule that divisible-by-2 means even plus the no-remainder check; evenness no longer follows. Does not claim 14 is odd, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: Rex is a dog.
to_premise_weak_hedge: Rex is a dog, and dogs have anatomical features typical of their kind.
notes: Removed the major premise (every dog has four legs); the leg count no longer follows. Does not deny four legs, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: Right now the sky is producing falling water droplets outdoors.
to_premise_weak_hedge: There are signs outdoors that can be associated with wet weather right now.
notes: Removed the definitional rule linking falling droplets to 'raining'; without the rule the conclusion is not entailed (could be sprinklers/spray). Does not assert it is dry, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: Maria is a 25-year-old citizen.
to_premise_weak_hedge: Maria is a citizen whose age and status are among the things that bear on voting eligibility.
notes: Removed the eligibility rule (citizen aged 18+ may vote); legal voting no longer follows. Does not say she is ineligible, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: The box and the book each have a weight.
to_premise_weak_hedge: The box and the book differ in size and heft in ways one might notice.
notes: Removed the specific masses (10 kg vs 1 kg); the comparison no longer follows. Does not assert the book is heavier, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: It is now 8 p.m., and the store keeps regular daytime business hours.
to_premise_weak_hedge: It is evening, and the store's hours are a factor in whether it is open right now.
notes: Removed the exact 9-to-5 window so 8 p.m. no longer entails closed (vague 'daytime' could extend later). Does not assert the store is open, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: The liquid in the beaker has a measured pH of 3.
to_premise_weak_hedge: The liquid's pH reading is among the properties relevant to classifying it chemically.
notes: Removed the rule (pH below 7 = acid); without it the bare pH=3 does not formally entail 'acid'. Does not call it a base, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: Anil, Brit, and Chen differ in age from one another.
to_premise_weak_hedge: There are age differences among Anil, Brit, and Chen worth comparing.
notes: Removed the two ordered relations (Anil>Brit>Chen) that chain to the conclusion; the Anil>Chen ordering no longer follows. Does not assert Chen is older, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: The shape is a triangle.
to_premise_weak_hedge: The shape is a triangle, with the geometric properties such figures generally have.
notes: Removed the defining rule (every triangle has exactly three sides); the side count no longer formally follows. Does not deny three sides, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: The team scored 3 goals to their opponent's 1.
to_premise_weak_hedge: The team's and opponent's goal tallies are among the things that bear on the result.
notes: Removed the rule that more goals wins; the bare scoreline no longer formally entails a win. Does not assert they lost, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: The metal sphere is denser than water.
to_premise_weak_hedge: The metal sphere's density relative to water is a factor in how it behaves when placed in water.
notes: Removed the rule (denser-than-water objects sink); sinking no longer follows from density alone. Does not say it floats, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: Folding a sheet of paper repeatedly adds to its overall thickness with each successive fold.
to_premise_weak_hedge: Repeatedly folding a sheet of paper can have a striking cumulative effect on how thick the stack becomes.
notes: Removed the doubling (2^42) mechanism and the arithmetic showing ~4.4e8 m; growth sounds plausibly linear/small, so reaching the Moon no longer follows. Does not deny large growth, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: With 23 people, the chance that all birthdays are distinct is found by multiplying a sequence of shrinking fractions, leaving the rest as the chance of a shared birthday.
to_premise_weak_hedge: Even fairly small groups of people can have a surprisingly high chance of a shared birthday.
notes: Removed the numeric result (~0.493 / ~0.507 crossing one half); without the number it is unclear the probability exceeds 50%. Does not claim it is below half, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: The number of orderings of a 52-card deck is 52!, an enormous figure, and Earth is made of a vast number of atoms.
to_premise_weak_hedge: The count of ways to order a deck of cards is famously huge, on a scale that invites comparison to astronomical quantities.
notes: Removed the specific magnitudes (~8e67 vs ~1e50) needed to rank one above the other; with both merely 'huge/vast' the ordering vs atom count no longer follows. Does not assert atoms exceed orderings, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: Braess's paradox concerns how adding a road to a network interacts with the routing choices that self-interested drivers make.
to_premise_weak_hedge: Adding a road can be among the factors that influence overall travel times in a congested network in non-obvious ways.
notes: Removed the equilibrium mechanism (selfish routing shifting the Nash equilibrium to over-use the shortcut and raise total time); a worse outcome no longer follows. Does not claim the road always helps, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: Simpson's paradox concerns how subgroup comparisons relate to the comparison drawn after pooling all patients together.
to_premise_weak_hedge: How data are grouped can be relevant to the apparent direction of a treatment comparison.
notes: Removed the mechanism (unequal subgroup sizes + confounder correlated with both, causing the pooled rates to reverse); a full reversal no longer follows. Does not deny reversals occur, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: In the Monty Hall game your first pick may be right or wrong, and after the host reveals a goat you choose whether to switch.
to_premise_weak_hedge: In the Monty Hall game, whether you switch can be relevant to your chance of winning the car.
notes: Removed the probability accounting (1/3 vs 2/3, host forced to reveal the only other goat) that yields the 2/3-vs-1/3 result; the specific doubling no longer follows. Does not claim staying is better or equal, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: Benford's law describes a tendency for smaller leading digits to appear more often than larger ones in certain real-world datasets.
to_premise_weak_hedge: In some datasets the distribution of leading digits is uneven, with smaller digits playing a notably larger role.
notes: Removed the formula log10(1+1/d) and the specific ~30.1% (d=1) vs ~4.6% (d=9) figures; the precise '1 about 30%, far more than 9' no longer follows. Does not deny the skew, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: The coastline paradox concerns how the length you measure for a coastline depends on the size of the measuring stick you use.
to_premise_weak_hedge: The choice of measuring scale can be among the factors affecting the length one records for a coastline.
notes: Removed the fractal mechanism (self-similar, dimension >1, perimeter grows without bound as ruler shrinks); 'larger and larger with no single true length' no longer follows. Does not assert a fixed convergent length, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: The Great Pyramid, Cleopatra, and the first Pizza Hut each belong to widely separated points in history.
to_premise_weak_hedge: The relative timing of Cleopatra, the Great Pyramid, and the first Pizza Hut is the sort of thing that can produce surprising comparisons.
notes: Removed the dates (2560 BCE, 30 BCE, 1958 CE) and the gap arithmetic (2530 vs 1988 years); which gap is smaller no longer follows. Does not assert the opposite ordering, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: Oxford as a place of teaching and the Aztec state each have their own founding history.
to_premise_weak_hedge: The respective origins of Oxford and the Aztec Empire are the kind of thing that invites historical comparison.
notes: Removed the dating (Oxford by 1096/late 12th c. vs Tenochtitlan 1325 / empire 1428); which is older no longer follows. Does not claim the Aztec Empire is older, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: DNA stores information chemically, and a gram of it contains a great many nucleotides; total global hard-drive capacity is also large.
to_premise_weak_hedge: DNA's information-carrying capacity is the sort of thing that can be relevant when comparing it to conventional digital storage.
notes: Removed the quantification (2 bits/nucleotide, ~1e21 nucleotides, ~2e20 bytes vs global capacity); the 'more than all hard drives combined' ranking no longer follows. Does not assert DNA stores less, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: The mantis shrimp's club strike creates collapsing cavitation bubbles that briefly release energy as light and heat in a tiny region.
to_premise_weak_hedge: The mantis shrimp's strike produces dramatic physical effects in the surrounding water that can include intense local conditions.
notes: Removed the temperature figure (thousands of K, compared to the sun's ~5800 K); the 'roughly the sun's surface temperature' claim no longer follows. Does not deny heating occurs, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: The Hafele-Keating experiment flew atomic clocks around the world and compared them with a ground clock to test relativity's predictions about moving clocks at altitude.
to_premise_weak_hedge: Flying an atomic clock can be relevant to how it reads relative to a clock left on the ground, according to relativity.
notes: Removed the confirmed-disagreement result (velocity and gravitational effects netting tens-to-hundreds of ns matching theory); a measurable disagreement no longer follows from a mere 'test'. Does not assert clocks agree, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: Bananas are rich in potassium, and potassium is an element with more than one naturally occurring isotope.
to_premise_weak_hedge: Bananas' potassium content is among the things that can be relevant to discussions of natural radioactivity.
notes: Removed the specific fact that ~0.012% of natural potassium is radioactive K-40 (the source of the emission); radioactivity no longer follows from 'has potassium with isotopes'. Does not say bananas are non-radioactive, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: Antarctica's interior is a cold desert and receives little precipitation, while parts of the Sahara also receive limited rain.
to_premise_weak_hedge: Both Antarctica and the Sahara include very dry regions, which makes their relative dryness an interesting comparison.
notes: Removed the extreme-case detail (Dry Valleys with essentially no precipitation for long periods being drier than wetter Sahara parts); the cross-over comparison no longer follows. Does not assert the Sahara is always drier, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: Honey has a high sugar concentration and acidic character, and jars of it have been recovered from ancient Egyptian tombs.
to_premise_weak_hedge: Honey's chemical makeup is among the factors relevant to how well it keeps over time.
notes: Removed the preservation mechanism (low water activity + high sugar + ~3.9 pH + hydrogen-peroxide enzyme jointly preventing microbial growth); 'still edible after millennia' no longer follows. Does not claim the honey spoiled, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: The Banach-Tarski theorem concerns partitioning a solid ball into pieces and moving them with rigid motions.
to_premise_weak_hedge: The Banach-Tarski theorem is among the results showing that infinite-set geometry can behave very counterintuitively.
notes: Removed the load-bearing details (axiom of choice, finitely many non-measurable pieces, rotations/translations reassembling into two identical copies); doubling the ball no longer follows. Does not deny the result, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: Zipf's law describes a regular relationship between a word's frequency and its frequency rank in natural language.
to_premise_weak_hedge: Word frequencies in natural language follow patterns that can persist in various ways across the vocabulary.
notes: Removed the inverse-rank quantification (freq proportional to 1/rank, giving rank-1 ~2x rank-2, ~3x rank-3, and scale-free persistence after stopword removal); the specific 2x/3x ratios after filtering no longer follow. Does not deny the pattern, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: There are 23 people on the field, the two eleven-player teams plus the referee.
to_premise_weak_hedge: The number of people on a soccer field, two teams and a referee, is the kind of group size that makes shared-birthday questions interesting.
notes: Removed the probability result (~0.493 all-distinct, ~0.507 crossing one half) for 23; that a match is more likely than not no longer follows. Does not assert it is unlikely, so not a contradiction.

to_premise_weak_quant: The Sun is very far from Earth and its light travels to us at the finite speed of light, so it takes some time to arrive.
to_premise_weak_hedge: The Sun's distance and the finite speed of light are factors in how long sunlight takes to reach us.
notes: Removed the figures (~1.496e11 m, ~3e8 m/s, ~499 s) that pin the delay to 'over eight minutes'; the specific >8-minute duration no longer follows. Does not assert instantaneous or shorter travel, so not a contradiction.
